title: Financial regulators need to get ahead of the curve on private credit
gist: Private credit has ballooned from ~$40bn in 2000 to $1.5-2tn (far larger on broad definitions), is now larger in nominal terms than 2007 subprime, yet remains lightly regulated and untested by a credit cycle. Growing links to banks, insurers and retail/401k investors mean it is on track to become systemically important, and regulators should build data access and cross-border stability architecture before a reckoning.

themes.theme: Scale and rapid growth of private credit

insights.claim: Global non-bank financial institution (NBFI) assets reached $257 trillion by end-2024, more than half of total global financial assets, per the FSB.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Shadow-banking now dominates the financial system, so risks incubating outside prudential bank regulation can be system-scale.
insights.quote: The assets within global Non-Bank Financial Institutions (NBFIs) increased to $257 trillion by the end of 2024
insights.locator: The dramatic rise in private credit

insights.claim: Private credit is around $1.5-2 trillion on a narrow definition but $10-50 trillion once distressed debt, asset-backed lending, commercial real estate, consumer and corporate finance are included.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The market's true size is deeply uncertain, so headline figures likely understate the exposure investors and regulators must model.
insights.quote: private credit can be far larger – in the $10–50 trillion range
insights.locator: Of that, private credit – that is

insights.claim: Private credit has grown from around $40 billion in 2000 and is already greater in nominal value than all outstanding subprime mortgages were in 2007.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The pre-2008 subprime analogy frames the scale of a potential blow-up and argues against complacency about the sector being 'modest'.
insights.quote: already greater in nominal value than all outstanding subprime mortgages in 2007
insights.locator: However, private credit has grown rapidly

insights.claim: The sector's growth was driven by 2009-2022 ultra-low rates plus tighter bank-lending regulation and the parallel rise of private equity.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The tailwinds that built the market are now reversing, so higher rates could stress borrowers the sector was built to serve.
insights.locator: This rapid growth took place during

insights.claim: Over 87 per cent of private credit originated in the US in 2025, with Europe, the UK and Canada making up most of the rest.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: US-centric origination means US regulatory choices and credit conditions drive the global risk picture.
insights.quote: Most private credit originates in the US (o ver 87 per cent in 2025 )
insights.locator: Most private credit originates in the US

themes.theme: Retail investor exposure and borrower credit quality

insights.claim: The retail share of private credit funds has risen from near zero to 13 per cent over the last decade, and the US Department of Labor proposed in March 2026 opening 401k accounts to alternatives like private credit.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Widening retail and pension access transmits an opaque, illiquid asset to unprotected savers, raising both political and systemic stakes.
insights.quote: the share of retail investors...in private credit funds has gone from near zero to 13 per cent in the last decade
insights.locator: But the share of retail investors

insights.claim: Private credit investors lack deposit insurance and the emergency liquidity support available to banks, while borrowers are concentrated in services, tech and healthcare, often unrated or clustered at junk B- ratings and more leveraged than syndicated-loan borrowers.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The combination of no safety net, weak credit quality and high leverage means losses in a downturn could be sharp and fall on unprotected holders.
insights.quote: Investors in private credit funds do not enjoy protections such as deposit insurance

insights.claim: Liquidity risk is rising as US funds increasingly offer periodic partial redemption windows attractive to retail investors wanting to cash out quickly.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Liquidity mismatch between redeemable fund shares and illiquid loans creates run risk that can force fire-sales in stress.

themes.theme: AI threat to key borrower sectors

insights.claim: One-fifth of private credit loans are to Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) companies whose business models are threatened by AI.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A concentrated bet on SaaS makes the loan book directly exposed to AI disruption of software incumbents.
insights.quote: One-fifth of private credit loans are to Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) companies whose business models are threatened by AI

insights.claim: Large declines in software and BDC stock prices have been partly driven by fund investors exiting through redemptions over AI fears.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: BDC share-price weakness is an early, observable market signal that the AI-vs-SaaS thesis is already prompting redemptions.
insights.locator: Large declines in software and BDC stock prices

themes.theme: Systemic linkages to banks, insurers and asset managers

insights.claim: Banks are linked to private credit through partnerships, lending to fund investors, direct fund investment, buying private CLOs, and synthetic risk transfers via credit-linked notes or CDS spreads.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Dense, opaque bank linkages mean private-credit stress can spill into the regulated banking system despite it sitting 'outside' banks.
insights.locator: Links between banks and private credit are numerous

insights.claim: US private equity-based insurers control nearly $900 billion in liabilities, up from $67 billion in 2012, after PE firms took controlling stakes in insurers that then invested in related private credit.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A 13x jump in PE-controlled insurance liabilities ties policyholder money to affiliated private credit, adding conflict-of-interest and contagion channels.
insights.quote: private equity-based insurers control nearly $900 billion in liabilities – up from $67 billion in 2012

insights.claim: Five large asset managers account for one-third of aggregate loan commitments in the private credit sector, and many run PE and private credit under the same roof.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Extreme concentration plus co-located PE/credit strategies raises conflict-of-interest risk and single-manager systemic importance.
insights.quote: five large asset managers account for one-third of aggregate loan commitments in the private credit sector

insights.claim: Private credit is likely to follow prior credit innovations (emerging market debt, high-yield bonds, subprime CDOs) through a reckoning, then improved regulation, then maturity into a regular asset class.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The base case is a crisis-then-reform cycle, so investors should expect a shakeout before the asset class stabilizes.
insights.locator: Private credit is likely to follow the path

insights.claim: The author argues the direction of travel means private credit will become systemically important, rejecting the view that risk is contained by its institutional base and modest size.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Treating private credit as already systemic changes the regulatory and hedging posture investors should adopt now, not after a blow-up.
insights.quote: the direction of travel...suggests private credit will become systemically important

insights.claim: The 2008 crisis was far worse than the ~$1.3 trillion of subprime mortgages implied because layers of leverage, CDOs of CDOs and outsized notional CDS magnified losses, and similar opaque leverage layers may make private credit's systemic risk larger than it appears.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Hidden leverage, not headline notional, drove 2008 losses, so private credit's true tail risk should be judged by its embedded leverage and linkages.
insights.quote: Similar opaque layers of leverage may make systemic risk from private credit significantly greater than it may appear
insights.locator: And the subprime crisis of 2008 demonstrated

insights.claim: Recommended fixes include standardizing private-credit definitions, coordinated international data access without overly onerous reporting, cross-border stability architecture, and requiring retail/401k-eligible funds to have diversified loans, liquidity provisions and high leverage transparency.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: These proposals flag the coming regulatory direction, signalling likely compliance costs and product design constraints for private-credit managers.
